title: Branzino al forno
subtitle: Whole sea bass baked with lemon, rosemary, and garlic
excerpt: A whole sea bass baked in a hot oven with lemon, garlic, rosemary, and a splash of white wine. The cavity is packed with aromatics; the wine creates steam that keeps the flesh moist. Crisp skin, juicy interior, barely 30 minutes start to finish.
sourceNotes: Baked whole fish in the Italian tradition is documented throughout coastal Italian cooking, with regional variations in the aromatics used. Artusi discusses baked fish preparations including sea bass in La scienza in cucina (1891, public domain). Branzino (European sea bass, Dicentrarchus labrax) is the fish most associated with this preparation in central and southern Italy; the Mediterranean-farmed variety is widely available outside Italy.

content.text: Cooking fish whole is easier than cooking fillets. A whole fish is far more forgiving. The bone keeps the flesh from drying out. The skin protects it from the oven's heat. The steam in the cavity keeps everything moist. The gap between undercooked and cooked is a few minutes, and the gap between cooked and overcooked is the same. But a whole fish is far harder to ruin than a skinless fillet in a pan.

Ask the fishmonger to gut and scale the fish if they have not already. Score the flesh through to the bone on each side before seasoning, this lets the aromatics penetrate and helps the heat reach the thickest part. The fish is done when the flesh at the thickest point behind the head flakes when prodded with a fork and is opaque all the way through.

items.name: Sea bass
items.prepNote: whole, about 600 to 700 g, gutted and scaled

items.name: Lemon
items.prepNote: 1 sliced into rounds, 1 halved for serving

content.text: Preheat the oven to 220°C (fan 200°C). Dry the {{sea-bass}} inside and out with kitchen paper. Using a sharp knife, cut three diagonal slashes through the skin to the bone on each side. Season generously with salt and pepper inside and out and in the slashes. Fill the cavity with the {{rosemary}} sprigs, the halved {{garlic}} cloves, and a few slices of {{lemon}}. Lay the remaining lemon slices and sliced garlic over the top of the fish.

Place the fish in a roasting tin. Drizzle the {{olive-oil}} of olive oil over the fish. Pour the {{white-wine-dry}} of white wine around it. Bake for 20 to 22 minutes until the skin is golden and slightly blistered and the flesh at the thickest point flakes when tested with a fork.

Rest for 5 minutes. Drizzle with the {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}} of extra-virgin olive oil. Bring to the table whole and fillet at the table, or lift the flesh from the bone in the kitchen. Serve with the halved lemon and any pan juices spooned over.

items.fix: Bring to room temperature for 20 minutes before baking; add 3 to 4 minutes for a larger fish
items.cause: Fish was very cold straight from the fridge, or thicker than expected
items.symptom: Fish is undercooked at the bone after 22 minutes

items.fix: Dry the skin thoroughly; make sure the oven is at full temperature before the fish goes in
items.cause: Fish not dried before cooking, or oven not fully preheated

items.fix: Check at 18 minutes for a 600 g fish; remove as soon as the flesh flakes at the thickest point
items.cause: Overcooked; sea bass cooks fast and the window for perfectly cooked is narrow
items.symptom: Flesh is dry and flaking into pieces

items.fix: Use at least 100 ml of wine; add a splash of water if the pan looks dry after 15 minutes
items.cause: Too little wine, or oven too hot for the size of the fish
items.symptom: Pan juices evaporated and fish stuck

content.text: Branzino al forno belongs to the coastal kitchens of central and southern Italy, from Tuscany to Campania. It also lives on the menus of Rome, served on a plain white dish with a wedge of lemon. European sea bass is now farmed in the Adriatic and in Greek and Turkish waters. It is one of the most widely sold whole fish in Europe. The farmed variety has a milder flavour than wild-caught. A 600 g farmed bass baked this way is still an excellent dinner: clean, light, and exactly the kind of cooking Italian seafood restaurants do best.
